• 🏛️ The Supreme Court has ruled that mining lease applicants with pre-2015 approvals and vested rights continue to retain their rights despite later amendments to mining laws. 

• 📜 The Court clarified that such cases would not be treated as pending applications in 2015 and hence are not subject to the auction regime introduced under Section 10A of the MMDR Act. 

• 🚫 The judgment also held that these rights are not affected by the 2021 lapse provisions. 

• 🏗️ Deccan Gold is examining the implications of the ruling on its Ganajur gold project, which has been under legal proceedings. 

• ⚖️ The Supreme Court dismissed the PIL challenging the mining lease grant and upheld that vested rights accrued before 2015 remain valid. 

🟢 Impact: Positive (The ruling could significantly strengthen Deccan Gold's legal position for the Ganajur project and improve prospects for securing mining rights without auction.)

*CITI on LG Electronics India*
Initiates coverage with Buy rating & TP of Rs 1,800
Dominant market share in washing machines, refrigerators, TVs, & ACs
85%+ localized, in-house manufacturing with strong parent R&D
Low domestic penetration offers structural growth runway
Upcoming Sri City plant involves Rs 50bn capex
Models 12% revenue & 23% PAT CAGR over FY26-29E

*UBS on Metal*
Hindalco – Initiate Buy with TP of Rs 1325
NALCO – Initiate Buy with TP of Rs 460
Coal India – Initiate Buy with TP of Rs 550
Jindal Steel – Initiate Neutral with TP of Rs 1300
Tata Steel – Initiate Neutral with TP of Rs 220
JSW Steel – Initiate Neutral with TP of Rs 1400
SAIL – Initiate Sell with TP of Rs 170
NMDC – Initiate Sell with TP of Rs 80
A mix of positive cyclical momentum and structural cautions
Sector poised for earnings growth, but valuations make us selective
Aluminium: favourable macros and operating leverage not fully priced in
Steel: cautious view, given limited upside to realizations or valuations
Coal as a structural beneficiary of India’s growing energy demand
NMDC faces headwinds: potential execution challenges, flattish iron ore pricing

*UBS on Lenskart*
Initiate Buy, TP Rs 730
Lenskart has emerged as an industry disruptor, delivering quality prescription eyewear with a sharp value-for-money positioning, supported by its tech-enabled vertically
integrated model
It offers products of comparable quality to peers at nearly half price, a value proposition many competitors may struggle to emulate
By pursuing this value innovation strategy, it has emerged as the dominant leader; its India division is nearly 6x larger than pioneer Titan
It delivers consol gross margins of 69%, & Pre-Ind-AS EBITDA margin of 11%, which expect to continue expanding as scale increases
Expect this to create exponential earnings growth, with an EPS CAGR of 44% over FY26-31E (64% in FY26).

*Macquarie on Phoenix Mills*
Initiate O-P. TP Rs 2100
Phoenix is India's largest pure-play name in premium urban consumption, operating 11mn sf of Grade A malls in eight cities
50% mall GLA expansion, 4x growth in office spaces, and 900 key hotel buildouts by FY30 underpin Phoenix's growth runway.
Incremental capex of Rs12 bn annually is funded through internal accruals. A
Catalysts:
1) Commissioning of pipeline malls through FY27-FY30;
2) completion of refurbishments at Palladium Mumbai, Marketcity Bangalore, and Marketcity Pune by FY27
3) office lease-up at Mall of Asia and Mall of the Millennium.

*Macquarie on GMR Airports*
Initiate O-P, TP Rs 125
GMR's India gateway airports are embedded with premium travel consumption.
Captive passenger ecosystems drive passenger spends through retail, duty free and integrated monetisation
Its large landbank offers upside through commercial development and ecosystem expansion
See an 18% Ebitda CAGR over FY26-29E

*Jefferies on GMR Airports*
Buy, TP Rs 125
Management meet takeaways
Three engines - aero (regulated returns), non-aero (now larger driver), and real estate - anchor growth.
Delhi’s hub ambitions with rising transfer traffic, mid teens non-aero growth and asset light consumer platform strategy were highlighted.
With capex moderating, FCF turning positive and leverage falling, BS is seeing inflection, with land monetisation highlighted as another key upside lever

*Jefferies on Interglobe Aviation*
Buy, TP Rs 5380
Management meet takeaways
Mgmt reiterated pragmatic approach with near-term focus on pricing over capacity push amid cost inflation.
Discussions also focused on long-term tailwinds for Aviation growth, cost optimization and fleet expansion.
Airline is expanding forex hedging. Int'l segment is a key growth lever with new fleet, new routes.
Co is expanding its Geo and customer profile with recent initiatives.

text:
During FY26, sector revenues rose 10% YoY to a record US$32bn, led by ARPU growth and strong momentum in B- and C-Circles.
Bharti Airtel led sector gains, adding revenue market share across most circles, supported by premiumization and rural expansion.
VIL’s revenue rose 5% YoY, but subscriber losses drove further share erosion.
Over FY26-28E, expect revenues to grow at 13% CAGR to US$41bn, assuming a 15% tariff hike in Dec-26.

*Jefferies on Utilities*
Weak FY25-26 demand saw slowdown in renewable energy (RE) awards tendering to 26 GW vs 38-41 GW seen in FY24-25.
FY27 till date has been low at just 500 MW.
Power demand recovery began from Dec. 2025 and rose in double-digits in May and June till date
Believe awards run-rate will rise as demand strength north of 5-6% should remain.
Interestingly, tendering in FY25-26 saw Standalone Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) rise meaningfully.
JSW Energy, Adani Energy Solutions & NTPC are top picks
JSWE and NTPC should re-rate as execution ramp-up drives earnings growth and leads to stock returns AESL is locked in for 20%+ visible medium term EBITDA CAGR, which should drive upside on delivery.

*Jefferies on India Financials*
RBI expected to issue FAQ on Foreign Branch Leveraging for FCNR-B deposits
Allows overseas branches to provide leverage for domestic FCNR-B deposits
Minimizes capital friction & curbs counterparty risks to boost dollar inflows
Global clients can lock in annual dollar IRR of 14-18% with large domestic banks
Select domestic banks enhanced FCNR-B card rates by 200-350bps

*UBS on Suzlon*
Buy, TP Rs 72
Investor day takeaways
Management highlighted a clear strategic pivot to an integrated renewable energy (RE) platform targeting installed base share of 40% in five years in wind (vs 33% currently)
As compared to solar manufacturing peers, Suzlon’s integrated RE strategy is relatively asset-light whereby it intends to leverage on upcoming robust integrated solar manufacturing capacities via strategic tie ups
EPC & DevCo model will ensure reduced risk and better predictability/control in projects while delivering wind farms to customers.

*UBS on Indian Hotels*
Buy, TP Rs 900
Management meet takeaways
Management highlighted resilient domestic demand as war-related disruption impacting international travel was making customers prefer domestic travel
IH's strong pipeline supports FY27 growth guidance
Expect this momentum to sustain in coming quarters, aided by a softer base, & see potential for positive earnings surprises.
IH has demonstrated consistent execution, delivering growth and profitability despite intermittent demand challenges
Remain constructive on IH, supported by robust demand trends & strong execution
FY27 pipeline of 60 hotel openings (5,000 keys) provides solid visibility.
Alongside management fee growth and new business traction, IH expects this to drive 5–7% non-like-for-like growth, with ARRs contributing balance

*UBS on Nestlé*
Maintains Neutral rating with a target price of CHF 80.00
Nestlé India issued notice by FSSAI over detection of larvae in Maggi packet
Nestlé India exposure stands at 2.2% of global sales
Past: 2015 Maggi recall in India caused 30bps drag on group organic growth
2015 recall also led to 10-20bps hit to the group's operating margin

*Nomura on Nestle India*
Maintains Buy rating with target price of Rs 1,500
FSSAI issued notice over detection of larvae in a Maggi packet
Nestle clarified complaint originated from an unverfied X account
Independent lab testing of same batch confirmed samples free of infestation
Co submitted comprehensive quality records to the FSSAI

Buy, TP Rs 1600
Announced launch of gBosulif (Bosutinib tabs) 400 mg in US, with 180- days exclusivity
Product has annual sales of USD214mn at company level
As per IQVIA, product experienced volume growth of 6% y-y in MAT April 2026.

*Nomura on Adani Ports*
Maintain Buy; Hike TP to Rs 2080 from Rs 1850
Remains well-poised to capitalise on favourable industry tailwinds
FY26 annual report analysis – Smooth sailing
Management expects healthy revenue/EBITDA CAGRs of 19%/18% over FY26-31E
Raise FY27-28F EBITDA by 1-2%; forecast 19% EBITDA CAGR over FY26-29F

*JPM on LIC Housing*
Neutral. TP Rs 590
LICHF’s loan growth guidance of 10-12% & disbursements of Rs780bn (up 17% y/y) is ambitious.
A shift in distribution model through channel partners should improve growth, but is value dilutive.
Calculations suggest incremental disbursements via partners run at a 40bps RoA loss
Further, chasing prime mortgage growth in a highly competitive environment will keep loan yields under pressure.
Management’s mixed delivery on meeting prior guidance keeps growth forecasts lower at 6.1 this year.
1QFY27 disbursement is most important near-term catalyst, & meeting this could drive a re-rating (guidance 15% y/y; JPMe 13% y/y).

*JP Morgan on Swiggy*
OW maintained; prefers Eternal over Swiggy
Toing (new platform) seeing traction among price-sensitive customers
Toing DAUs jumped since Mar-26, reaching same as Swiggy & Zomato in 4 months
Core Swiggy & Zomato DAUs remain stable, showing no visible churn yet
Organic downloads for Toing surged to reach same level as Swiggy
Paid downloads rose since March, peaking at 6x Zomato in May-26 & currently at 2x
Risk of core platform cannibalization due to lower menu prices & 0 platform fees
Toing has no delivery fee above Rs 125 & flat Rs 25 fee for orders under Rs 125

*CLSA on Cement sector*
Post correction over past three months, Indian cement stocks are now trading at c. 1SD below the historical median and implying no profitability improvement even in FY28 (vs exit FY26). While believe FY27 is likely to be soft due to cost pressures and muted demand growth, as war risk subsides, expect profitability to jump in FY28
See value across most stocks in sector but prefer Ultratech, Dalmia and Shree given a better growth outlook.

*CLSA on India IT*
Muted hiring outlook as AI shifts requirements to human + AI agents
Naukri Jobspeak Index for IT services fell 7.3% YoY in May 2026
Strong 79% correlation with headcount additions of covered IT firms
BPO sector faces high risk of AI disruption; Jobspeak growth decelerated to 8.8% YoY
Major firms holding onto entry-level fresher pipelines despite lateral hiring slowdown

*Morgan Stanley on Reliance Industries*
Maintain Overweight, Maintain target price ₹1,803
Analyst Top Pick
Energy security policies and tighter refining markets seen keeping product spreads structurally stronger for longer
Oil to chemicals earnings supported despite higher logistics costs; company well placed on heavy and sour crude processing and diversified sourcing
Chemical cycle recovery aided by advantaged feedstocks through US ethane and captive naphtha expected to lift earnings 6-8% this year
Monetisation 4.0 underway as solar module, cell and energy storage manufacturing ramps up; not fully reflected in valuations

Iran's Mehr releases details of 14-article draft memorandum of understanding between Iran and US

1. Permanent and immediate end to war on all fronts, including Lebanon.

2. US commitment to non-interference in Iran's internal affairs and respect for the sovereignty of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

3. Complete lifting of the naval blockade within 30 days.

4. US commitment to withdraw its forces from around Iran.

5. Reopening of the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days, under Iranian arrangements.

6. Suspension of sanctions on oil and petrochemical product sales and derivatives, and full Iranian access to its financial resources.

7. Reconstruction plans to be presented by the US and its allies, totalling at least $300 billion.

8. A 60-day negotiation period to reach a final agreement on the nuclear issue and the complete removal of US primary and secondary sanctions, UN Security Council resolutions, and IAEA Board of Governors resolutions.

9. Reaffirmation of Iran's NPT commitment not to produce nuclear weapons.

10. During the negotiation period, the US commits not to add forces to the region and not to impose new sanctions.

11. Release of $24 billion in Iran's blocked funds during the 60-day final negotiation period, with half of that amount to be made available to Iran before negotiations begin.

12. Establishment of a monitoring mechanism to implement the agreement.

13. The final agreement to be ratified by a UN Security Council resolution.

14. Final negotiations will not begin until half of Iran's blocked funds are released, oil sanctions are suspended, and the naval blockade is lifted. The final agreement will address only the fate of enriched materials and enrichment, sanctions relief, and Iran's economic reconstruction programme. Talks on Iran's missile programme and its support for Resistance groups have been definitively removed from the agenda.

South Korea Eyes MSCI Developed-Market Upgrade After Surge In Equities

• 🇰🇷 South Korea is awaiting MSCI's annual market review on June 23, which could mark the first step toward its reclassification from Emerging Market (EM) to Developed Market (DM) status.

• 🚀 The KOSPI has surged over 90% in 2026, making it one of the world's best-performing major indices, driven largely by AI-linked stocks.

• ⏳ Most strategists expect MSCI to retain Korea in the EM category for now, but believe an eventual upgrade is becoming increasingly likely as market reforms take hold.

• 🌍 South Korea currently accounts for ~23% of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, making it the second-largest constituent.

• 💰 According to BNP Paribas, DM inclusion could attract around US$30 billion of passive inflows from benchmark-tracking funds.

• 📉 An upgrade could also narrow the long-standing "Korea Discount", which has historically kept Korean stocks trading at lower valuations than developed-market peers.

• 🏛️ Reclassification may attract a broader pool of long-term investors focused on governance, sustainability and shareholder returns.

• 🔄 Despite strong market gains, foreign investors have withdrawn over US$78 billion from Korean equities this year, highlighting the need for a more stable investor base.

🇮🇳 IMPACT ON INDIA

🔴 Near-term Negative: If South Korea moves toward developed-market status, passive flows could gradually shift from EM funds, potentially reducing India's weight in the MSCI EM index.

🟢 Long-term Positive: A Korean upgrade could eventually increase India's relative importance within EM indices, attracting larger allocations from emerging-market funds over time.

The way I see it:

The EV story isn't just about who assembles the battery.

It's about who controls the chemistry inside it.

China understood that years ago.

India is only now starting to build that capability.

DMC.

NMP.

Electrolyte salts.

Graphite anodes.

These sound like boring chemical products.

But they may end up being some of the most important pieces of India's EV supply chain over the next decade.

And that's the part of the story most investors still aren't watching.

And BAL isn't alone.

Across India, a battery materials ecosystem is quietly taking shape:

🧪 Gujarat Fluorochemicals → LiPF6 & PVDF

🧪 Neogen → electrolytes & lithium salts

🧪 Acutaas → electrolyte additives

🧪 Tatva Chintan → specialty electrolyte salts

🧪 HEG → synthetic graphite anodes

Different products.

Same theme.

Replace imports.

Build domestic capability.

Capture more of the EV value chain.

This is where the market starts paying attention.

A company that controls critical battery chemicals has a very different position than a commodity manufacturer.

Once a battery chemical gets qualified, switching suppliers isn't easy.

Testing takes time.

Approvals take time.

Customers hate changing formulations.

That's where the moat comes from.

Not capacity.

Chemistry.

What's even more interesting is what sits next to DMC.

BAL also manufactures NMP.

Every lithium ion battery needs it during electrode manufacturing.

Whether it's an LFP battery.

Whether it's NMC.

NMP is part of the process.

BAL produces it using its own upstream chemicals.

So you're not looking at a single product story.

You're looking at a company slowly embedding itself deeper into the EV battery supply chain. 🔋

Take Balaji Amines.

Until last year, India imported virtually all of its battery grade DMC requirement.

Mostly from China.

DMC is a critical solvent used inside lithium ion battery electrolytes.

In May 2025, BAL commissioned its electronic grade DMC facility at Solapur.

Just like that, India got its first domestic source.

No big announcement.

No hype.

Just import substitution happening in real time.

Everyone wants to build battery gigafactories.

Very few are talking about the guys supplying the chemistry.

That's where the economics get interesting.

Cell manufacturing globally is fighting overcapacity and weak utilization.

Battery chemicals?

Higher margins.

Long qualification cycles.

Sticky customers.

And India is finally building that layer.

🇨🇳 China doesn't just make EV batteries.

It controls the chemicals that make EV batteries possible.

80% of global graphite supply.

~90% of anode and cathode material manufacturing.

Dominance across lithium, cobalt and graphite refining.

Which means every time India sells an EV, someone in China's chemical chain gets paid.

India's investment rate is expected to go from 34.6% of GDP today to 37.5% by FY30.

Last time such a capex cycle was seen in 2004-08 period but the nature of capex this time is completely different 

And one can divide this into 3 buckets - 

1) Well-Entrenched (money already flowing): Coal, Renewables, Transmission, Defence, Railways, Oil & Gas

2) Uptick Evidence (gaining momentum): Data Centers, Semis, Green Equipment, BESS

3) Emerging (early innings, high optionality): Nuclear, Water, Shipbuilding, Coal Gasification, Carbon Capture

Coal is not a legacy constraint. India hit a peak demand record of 271 GW on May 21, 2026 for the 4th consecutive all-time high. Thermal anchored the grid at ~63% of generation. At night, ~190 GW of coal ran at near-full capacity with shortages still touching 5.4 GW. NTPC is adding 13 GW. BHEL and L&T are reporting record order inflows.

India added a record 44.6 GW solar + 6 GW wind in FY26. Impressive. But CERC's new Deviation Settlement Mechanism (Apr-26) is quietly ending the "Must Run" free pass for RE generators. By 2031, solar/wind will face the same grid scheduling penalties as coal plants. Standalone RE is dead. RE + Storage is the only viable model going forward. Every new project will need BESS embedded.

BESS could be the defining investment theme of India over the next decade. Battery costs down 70% in 3 years. 117 GWh tendered cumulatively. CEA targets 80 GW by FY36. India is still 90% import-dependent on China for the battery value chain. One needs to check up on how the value chain gets created. 

The HVDC cycle is just starting. RE generation is concentrated (Rajasthan: 119 GW, South India: 106 GW). Demand is everywhere else.  ±800 kV HVDC = 6 GW bulk transfer over 1,000–2,000 km. India needs 120–130 GW across ~22 corridors. Current installed: ~18 GW. That's a 6x scale-up. Combined CEA + Brahmaputra Basin capex = Rs14.3 trillion in transmission alone. GE Vernova, Hitachi Energy, Siemens Energy sit at the centre of this (Around them is a big value chain that is there). India has no fully integrated domestic HVDC OEM yet.

Nuclear  the structural shift is real this time. SHANTI Act passed. 60-year state monopoly dismantled. FDI up to 49% allowed. PFBR achieved first criticality in April 2026 India entered Stage 2 of its nuclear programme. Target: 8.8 GW today → 100 GW by 2047. ~$200bn cumulative opportunity. Maharashtra alone signed nuclear MoUs worth Rs6.5 trillion with Reliance, NTPC, Adani, and others.
L&T targeting 3x nuclear revenue in 5 years. BHEL can currently supply 30–40% of a nuclear plant's bill of materials.

Also semiconductor ecosystem is getting created. 6 months ago this was all policy announcements but Micron ATMP inaugurated. Kaynes OSAT running. CG Power scaling. Tata-ASML MoU signed for Dholera fab (28nm–110nm nodes, 50,000 wafers/month). First sellable wafers expected: late 2026. India's semis market: $25–30bn today → $ 110bn by 2030. ISM 2.0 launched.

And then there is the DC boom that is coming to India. India's installed DC capacity: 1.8 GW → 10.5 GW by FY31 (AI: 6.8 GW, non-AI: 3.7 GW). Microsoft: $ 17.5bn. Google-AdaniConneX: $ 15bn. AWS: $ 15.3bn combined. Reliance-Brookfield-Digital Realty JV: $ 11bn. ~$75bn announced in 5 years. Hyperscalers are treating India as a geopolitical hedge which has stable democracy, growing digital market, coal baseload + RE mix. Power systems alone = $ 20bn opportunity for industrials.

This isn't a government-funded capex cycle hoping private capital shows up.

Corporate debt/GDP: 52% (vs 61% pre-pandemic). Industrial credit growth: 15% YoY. Bank NPAs: 2.5% at multi-year lows. Gross FDI: $ 94.5bn in FY26, +17% YoY.

Private capex to grow at ~16% CAGR through FY31. Investment rate peaks at ~37.5% of GDP.

Source - Morgan Stanely

Bernstein On Autos

India Autos: The 350cc battle redrawn?

Bajaj Triumph was positioned in the 400cc segment trying to build a new sub category in the premium motorcycle segment

But the GST cuts on sub-350cc motorcycles pushed Bajaj to migrate its Triumph lineup from 398cc to 349cc, placing it in the same engine segment as Royal Enfield

Royal Enfield’s demand is unambiguous - 3 month waiting periods on standard models vs Triumph is delivering within forty-eight hours

What Triumph has not yet solved is equally clear. Beyond the top 8-10 cities, it is largely absent

Royal Enfield’s grip on the Classic and Bullet buyer- rooted in community, habit, and cultural identity is not easily disrupted
